segments.s695-g121: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. عنوان آن نامه is a noun phrase 'the heading/title of that letter' (the ezāfe linking is implied in poetry: 'عنوانِ آن نامه'). برگشت is simple past 3sg 'came back/was returned', and خشک is a post-verbal adjective/adverb modifying برگشت — 'came back dry/bare' (i.e. without the expected marking).
segments.s695-g122: برو here functions as a brief narrative particle 'thereupon/then' (not a literal imperative) leading into the main clause. برنهادند is past plural 'they placed/raised/produced' (poetic past of نهادن). مهری زمشک is an object phrase: مهری = 'a little seal/mark (from مهر)' and زمشک qualifies it (material or type: 'barberry'), with the noun–modifier order characteristic of Persian poetry.
segments.s695-g123: بران is an adverbial 'thereupon/on that' linking back to the previous action. مهر here means 'seal/stamp' (the object placed). بنهاد is past 3sg 'he put/placed/set' (poetic past of نهادن); the subject قیصر follows the verb (a common poetic word order) and is the actor 'the Caesar'. نگین is the noun 'gem/signet' which will be taken up in the next clause as what is given.
segments.s695-g124: فرستاده 'the messenger' is the direct object; را is the definite-object marker. داد is past 3sg 'gave' (so قیصر is the giver). وکرد links to the following predicate and functions as 'and did/and uttered'; آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise, acclamation' — together کردن آفرین = 'to praise/utter "bravo"'.
segments.s695-g1: هم آنگه is a temporal phrase: هم (an adverbial intensifier) + آنگه (then), so together it means "then/at that moment." یکی نامه بنوشت is a simple SOV clause typical of Classical Persian: یکی (an indefinite 'someone') is the subject, نامه (object) follows, and بنوشت is the past tense verb 'wrote' (here the printed lemma is odd; context shows it functions as past of نوشتن). زود is an adverb placed after the verb meaning 'quickly/soon', modifying how the writing was done.
segments.s695-g2: بران introduces 'thereupon' or 'on that' and links this clause to the previous action. آفرین آفرین is a repeated interjection ('Afarin!' = 'bravo!'/praise) used as an exclamation; the repetition intensifies it. بر + فزود is a prepositional/verb sequence where بر ('upon') precedes the verb فزود (past 'increased'/'grew'), so literally 'upon that he increased (the praise),' i.e. he heightened or added to the praise.
segments.s695-g3: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'namely'). با موبد is a prepositional phrase 'with the mobed' (mōbed = Zoroastrian priest). یکدل و پاک رای is a compound nominal phrase modifying the participants: یکدل 'of one heart, united', و پاک 'and pure' qualify رای, here 'counsel/decision' — the adjectives appear before the noun they describe, typical in poetry.
segments.s695-g4: زدیم is the 1st-person-plural past of زدن used idiomatically with رای to mean 'we made/struck a decision.' از here introduces the domain or separation 'from' (i.e. distinguishing), followed by بد و نیک 'bad and good' (coordinated nouns). ناباک is an adjective 'chaste/virtuous' qualifying رای; the final رای repeats the noun 'counsel/decision.' Word order places the verb زدیم before the prepositional/nominal phrase it governs, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s695-g5: ز هرگونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'from every kind' (هرگونه‌ای = 'any kind/whatever sort'), followed by داستانها (plural object 'the tales/stories'). زدیم here is used with داستانها to mean 'we recited/performed/told' (زدن + داستان = to relate a tale); in poetic usage زدن can mean 'to produce/recite.' The verb comes after the object in this poetic SOV order.
segments.s695-g6: بران 'thereon' or 'on that' resumes the connection to the previous clause. رای here best reads as 'counsel/plan/opinion' and is followed by پیشینه 'the past/history' to form the phrase 'the counsel/history of former times.' باز آمدیم is a compound past 'we returned' (باز = again, آمدیم = we came/returned), so the clause means 'we returned to the earlier counsel/history.'
segments.s695-g7: کنون 'now' sets the time. رای و گفتارها is a coordinated NP 'opinions and sayings/utterances.' شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/happened' indicating that these things came into being. ببن is an imperative or interjection meaning 'see!' or 'behold!' placed after the verb for dramatic/poetic effect.
segments.s695-g8: گشادم here is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'I opened' or 'I have opened' (poetic past), followed by در گنجهای کهن 'in the ancient treasures/treasure-houses' which is the locative phrase indicating where the opening or disclosure took place. Word order places the verb before the locative complement, typical in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s695-g57: شما را places the object 'you' fronted and marked by the object marker را for emphasis ('as for you'). هر آنگه که is a fixed classical/poetic conditional/temporal connector meaning 'whenever' or 'whenever it happened that'. کاری is the noun 'a matter/affair' (here the thing whose occurrence is referred to) and بود is the past copula 'there was' — together: 'whenever there was any matter concerning you.'
segments.s695-g58: وگر introduces an alternative conditional 'or if / but if'. ناسزا here means an ill‑omened/insulting or improper thing (an offensive act) used substantively, modified by کارزاری 'of a campaign/warlike' — together meaning 'or if it was an offensive (ناسزا) warlike (کارزاری) affair'. بود is again the past copula 'was'.
segments.s695-g59: همه modifies the following predicate and indicates 'all / everyone'. دوستدار and برادر are predicates 'friends/supporters' and 'brothers' (comrades), joined by و. شویم is the 1st person plural present/optative form 'we become' or 'let us be / we would be' — here expressing the hoped-for or habitual state 'we would be all friends and brothers.'
segments.s695-g60: بود is the past/copular 'it was' introducing the remark that follows; نیز 'also' adds contrast. گاهی 'sometimes' + که introduces a subordinate clause 'that (it happened) that...'. کهتر is a comparative/adjective 'lesser/inferior' and شویم (again 1pl) 'we became' — 'it was also sometimes that we became lesser.'
segments.s695-g61: چو is the classical temporal conjunction 'when'. گردید is a past/resultative verb 'became/turned' (3rd sg). زین here functions as a prepositional particle (classical spelling of ز این = 'of/from this') and شهر ما 'our city/town' is the subject that becomes بی‌نیاز 'self-sufficient/untroubled' — 'when our city became self-sufficient.'
segments.s695-g62: به + دل‌تان locates the mental state 'in your hearts' (به marks direction/target). همه 'all' quantifies کینه 'hatred/grudge' as the thing that آید ('comes' / arises). فراز is an adverbial 'up/on high' meaning 'rise up' — literally 'all hatred comes up into your hearts.'
segments.s695-g63: ز ... و ز ... is the poetic 'from/of Tur and of Salm' (here names: تور and سلم). اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came forth / arose' and سخن is the noun 'speech/talk/story' — together: 'speech arose from/about Tur and Salm.' Note: classical word order places the source first (ز تور و ز سلم) and the verb اندر آمد after it.
segments.s695-g64: ازان is a classical contracted form 'from that / of those' linking the speech to what follows. بیهوده qualifies روزگار as 'futile/pointless' or 'vain', روزگار کهن 'the old/ancient times' — together: 'about those vain (or worthless) ancient days.'
segments.s695-g81: بماند is a jussive/optative 3rd-person form of ماندن ('may it remain' / 'let it remain') expressing a wish; the preposition ز ('from') links پیوند پیمان ('the bond of covenant') to what should remain, with ما ('our') marking possession after the noun (postposed possessive pronoun is normal in Persian). Word order is verb + prepositional phrase + possessed noun phrase.
segments.s695-g82: ز یزدان ('from/by God') is a prepositional phrase giving source or authority; چنین است فرمان ما is a verbless or copular clause where چنین ('thus') modifies فرمان ما ('our decree/command') and است ('is') simply links them — literal order 'thus is our command'. The whole segment functions as an authoritative gloss on what should remain.
segments.s695-g83: ز هنگام ('from the time of') introduces a temporal span; پیروز and خوشنواز are used as personal names/titles here (the construction 'A تا B' marks the interval 'from A until B'). تا is the subordinating preposition 'until' linking the two endpoints.
segments.s695-g84: همانا ('verily') is an emphatic particle. که introduces the content clause 'that a long year passed' (here functioning like English 'that' / 'for'), بگذشت is the past tense verb 'passed' governing the time noun سال دراز ('a long year'). The phrase is a simple past temporal statement following the temporal heading.
segments.s695-g85: The clause begins with کہ ('that/for'), introducing the consequence/explanation: سرها بدادند literally 'they gave heads' (بدادند past 'they gave'), an idiom meaning they forfeited their heads (were beheaded) and هر دو بباد ('each/both were blown away' — here 'بباد' = 'be lost') emphasizes that both perished. Word order is subject implied → object (سرها) → verb (دادند) → result phrase.
segments.s695-g86: جهاندار پیمان‌شکن خود مباد is an optative/jussive blessing/curse: جهاندار ('sovereign') modified by پیمان‌شکن ('oath-breaker'); خود here is a reflexive/intensive 'himself/his own'; and مباد is a fixed negative optative meaning 'may it not be' (i.e. 'may the sovereign not be an oath-breaker himself'). The construction places the wished-for negative at the end (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s695-g87: مسیح پیمبر is a title phrase 'Christ the Prophet' (noun + appositive/title). چنین کرد یاد is a somewhat archaic verbal phrase where کرد ('did/caused') combines with یاد ('remembrance') to mean 'thus caused remembrance' or more simply 'thus recalled/said'; the adverb چنین ('thus') modifies the action. The verb+noun collocation is poetic and equivalent to 'thus he said/remembered.'
segments.s695-g88: که introduces the reported content ('that ...'). پیچد is a present/subjunctive-form meaning 'turns' or 'is turned' and خرد ('the intellect/wisdom') is the subject; چون here functions temporally ('when' / 'as') and به پیچی زداد is a small noun phrase with به ('to/with') + پیچی ('a turn') and زداد intended as a past verbal form 'gave' (classical usage 'to give a turn'); together the clause says 'that the intellect turns when it has been given a turn.' Note: the final lexical item زداد is not covered by the senses list provided here, so its sense is unknown in the supplied inventory.
segments.s695-g33: اگر introduces a conditional protasis (“if”). مرد رومی is a noun phrase (noun + adjective) meaning “a Roman man.” به دل کین گرفت is an idiomatic verb phrase: گرفتن with the locative phrase به دل + object کین = “to take/harbor hatred in (one’s) heart.” گرفت is simple past (3rd singular) of گرفتن and completes the protasis.
segments.s695-g34: نباید is an impersonal modal negative meaning “it should not / ought not.” که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. آید is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used after نباید to express that something should not happen; تو را is the second‑person pronoun with the object marker را (poetic ordering places the clitic after the pronoun for emphasis). آن شگفت is the demonstrative + noun “that wonder/astonishment” which is the thing that should not befall you.
segments.s695-g35: خود آزردنی is a verbal noun/infinitival construction (آزردنی = “to offend/hurt”), with خود marking reflexive/oneself. نیست negates the copula (‘is not’). در دین ما is a prepositional phrase “in our religion,” so the clause literally says “hurting oneself is not in our religion.”
segments.s695-g36: مبادا introduces a negative optative/precautionary clause (“may it not be / lest”). بدی کردن is a gerundial phrase (بدی + کردن = “doing evil”); here کردن functions as the light verb forming the action. آیین ما (“our custom/practice”) is the predicative nominal; the copula is omitted (understood), so the line means “may doing evil never become our custom.”
segments.s695-g37: ندیدیم is 1st plural past “we did not see.” In negative contexts the object چیزِی follows the verb as an indefinite (“anything / something”)—here ندیدیم چیزی = “we saw nothing.” به از is a comparative construction meaning “better than” and is followed by راستی (“uprightness/honesty”), so: “we saw nothing better than uprightness.”
segments.s695-g38: همان is a resumptive/demonstrative pronoun meaning “that” or “namely,” pointing back to راستی. دوری is a noun here meaning “avoidance.” از introduces the things avoided (کژی و کاستی = “wrongness and deficiency”). The clause explicates that uprightness consists in avoiding crookedness and lack.
segments.s695-g39: ستمدیدگان is a plural noun “the oppressed” (those who sought help). را is the object marker placed after the noun, and همه quantifies it (“all”). خواندم is 1st singular past; here it most naturally takes the sense “I called/summoned” or “I summoned them,” so the line reads “I summoned all the oppressed.”
segments.s695-g40: وزین is an adjective/adverb meaning ‘weighty’ or ‘dignified’ modifying سخن. در فراوان is an adverbial phrase “in abundance / at length.” سخن راندم is an idiomatic use of راندن with سخن (literally “drove speech”), meaning “I uttered / I spoke”; راندم is past (1st singular). The whole clause: “and with weighty words I spoke much.”
segments.s695-g65: This clause is an elliptical, somewhat archaic word order meaning 'now one pledge must be firm.' یکی (one) precedes the noun عهد (pledge/oath). باید is a modal verb 'must/ought' that governs the clause, and کنون (now) + استوار (steady/firm) form the predicate/adverbial phrase describing how the pledge should be. The whole reads as a jussive/optative statement (an imperative-like necessity) rather than a simple description.
segments.s695-g66: سزاوار introduces a complement that characterizes the pledge: 'deserving/fit for ...' The noun phrase مهری یادگار functions as the thing for which it is deserving: 'a memento/token of affection.' The middle item برو here is poetic/elliptical and does not correspond to the simple verbal sense 'go' — it behaves like an archaic/localized element (likely contraction of برِ او / برای او or a linking particle) that links 'مهری' and 'یادگار' — so treat it as an idiomatic connector in this phrase.
segments.s695-g67: کزین باره is an adverbial phrase 'from this occasion' (کز = از + این in older/poetic spelling). از marks the topic/object of speech here: 'about/ concerning.' کین ایرج is a genitival phrase 'the grudge/vengeance regarding Irāj.' سخن is the noun 'speech/words' that will be negated by the following verb. Word order (prepositional/topic phrase + object noun) is typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s695-g68: نرانیم is a first-person plural jussive/imperative negative form meaning 'let us not utter/let us not put forth' (here 'do not speak'). The verb negates the preceding noun phrase ('سخن'), so the clause means 'let us not speak.' و ... از روزگار کهن continues the object: 'and (let us not speak) of the ancient times,' with از used as 'about/concerning' and کهن qualifying روزگار ('ancient times').
segments.s695-g69: ازین پس is the adverbial phrase 'from now on' (از + این + پس, here split orthographically). یکی باشد is an optative/subjunctive sense 'let (them) be one' or a future-oriented 'shall be one,' with باشد functioning as a jussive/potential copula. ایران و روم are the two coordinated subjects ('Iran and Rome'), so the clause expresses a desire/decision that the two be united.
segments.s695-g70: جدایی نجوییم is a negative jussive: جدایی (separation) is the object and نجوییم ('let us not seek' / 'we will not seek') is first-person plural subjunctive meaning 'we will not desire/pursue.' زین مرز و بوم is a locative/genitive phrase 'of/from this border and land' — زین is a poetic/contracted form corresponding to 'از این' (hence the sense 'of this'), linking the refusal to seek separation specifically from this frontier and homeland.
segments.s695-g71: پس is a connective 'then/therefore.' پرده ما ('our curtain/household') is the subject phrase; in epic/poetic Persian پرده often stands for the women's quarters or the household behind a veil. یکی دخترست is a copular predicate: یکی (one) + دختر ست (دختر است 'is a daughter') — note the archaic contraction -ست for است, so the clause states 'there is one daughter in our household.'
segments.s695-g72: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' از here marks the comparison source 'than/from the elders.' مهتران is a poetic plural meaning 'the greater/elders/seniors' (an older word for senior figures). برخرد is a compound adjective 'برخرد' = 'possessing superior wisdom / wise'; بهترست is a predicate 'is better / is superior.' Together the relative clause says the daughter 'is wiser/better of judgment than the elders.'
segments.s695-g49: بگویی is an optative/subjunctive second‑person form meaning roughly 'you would say' or 'say (that)'; که introduces the reported content. تا here means 'until' and takes a subjunctive clause; من is the emphatic first‑person subject placed before the predicate; بوم شهریار is a genitive phrase 'the ruler's bōm/land' (بوم = homeland/abode, شهریار = king/ruler).
segments.s695-g50: نگیرم is first‑person subjunctive/negative ('I will not take/accept' or 'I shall not be taken by'); with تا it yields 'until I (do not) ...' but here idiomatically 'until I take/secure ...' meaning 'I will not do X until I have done Y'. چنین رنجها = 'such hardships' (چنین = 'such'), and سست وخوار are descriptive adjectives 'weak and humiliated/despicable' modifying رنجها.
segments.s695-g51: نخواهم is a negative present/subjunctive of 'خواستن' meaning 'I will not/wish not to'; the pronoun من is emphatic. از رومیان = 'from the Romans' (source), باژ here means 'shout/acclaim/clamour' (a brief noun), and نیز ('also' / 'even') stresses that not even that is wanted.
segments.s695-g52: نه بفروشم is the negative subjunctive/intentional form of فروختن: 'I will not sell.' این رنجها را is the direct object marker را on 'these hardships'. بچیز is an archaic adverb meaning roughly 'for nothing/for any price' (here 'for anything'), so the whole clause means 'nor will I sell these hardships for anything.'
segments.s695-g53: دگر 'other / moreover', هرچ 'whatever/whatever thing', دارید second‑person plural/polite present of داشتن 'you have/possess'. زان = 'of that / from that', and مرز و بوم is a coordinated noun phrase 'border and land' (مرز = frontier, بوم = homeland). Word order places the quantifier + verb before the locative phrase.
segments.s695-g54: از ایران = 'from Iran'; کسی with a negative verb context means 'no one' (here used in the sense 'let no one'); نسپرد is the jussive/negative form of سپردن 'to hand over / entrust' ('may not hand over'); مرز روم = 'the border to Rome' (i.e. surrendering the frontier to Rum/Byzantium).
segments.s695-g55: بدین = 'by/with this', آرزو = 'wish/desire'. نیز here functions as 'also / furthermore'. بیشی کنید uses بیشی (comparative/augmentative 'more, greater') with the plural imperative/optative کنيد 'do' — together meaning 'do more on this' or 'pursue this hope further.'
segments.s695-g56: بسازید is an imperative/optative plural of ساختن 'to make/build' used idiomatically as 'be reconciled / settle (matters)'; با ما 'with us'; و 'and'; خویشی کنید uses خویشی 'kinship/affinity' with کنيد to mean 'form kinship / make yourselves kin' (i.e. become related/allied).
segments.s695-g89: بسی (an adverb) + چاره (noun) + کرد (past-tense verb) is an inverted word order where the quantity/degree and object precede the verb: 'devised many a remedy/plan'. اندران is a locative adverbial 'among them' modifying the action, and خوشنواز is the agent (an epithet 'the pleasant/minstrel-like one') placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s695-g90: که introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that'. پیروز is the person referred to ('the victor'); را is the Persian definite/object/topic marker attached after the possessor to mark the following noun as definite (here creating 'the victor's head'). سر is the noun 'head'; نیاید is the negative form of آمدن used here in the sense of 'should not come / not befall' (jussive/result sense). به + گاز gives the instrument/agental sense 'by/through a (harmful) bite/strike' — together: 'so that the victor's head would not come to harm by a blow.'
segments.s695-g91: چو here means 'when'. پیروز is the subject 'Pirooz'; با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him' (indicating the person toward whom the action was directed). درشتی is the noun 'harshness / rough conduct' functioning as the object of نمود; نمود is the past tense of 'to show/do' used here to mean 'acted / behaved (in such a way)'. The clause thus reads 'When Pirooz acted harshly with him...'.
segments.s695-g92: بدید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' placed at the start of the clause (poetic inversion). اندران is the locative 'in/among them' (here 'there'); جایگه is 'place/spot'; تیره is the adjective 'dark/ominous' qualifying دود 'smoke'. Together they form 'he saw there a place of dark smoke' with noun phrase order 'جایگه تیره دود' (place + adjective + noun) typical of poetic compression.
segments.s695-g93: شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was' introducing the result: 'it happened that'. آن لشکر 'that army' and تخت شاهی 'the royal throne / kingship' form the compound subject(s), و links the two items, and بباد is a poetic compound meaning 'went to the wind / was swept away / was lost' — together: 'That army and the kingship were swept away.'
segments.s695-g94: بپیچد is from پیچیدن 'to turn' and here narratively indicates turning away/flight ('they turned' / 'a rout occurred'). و 'and' links the two clauses. شد again marks 'it happened' and introduces the following result clause with شاه را (شاه marked by را as definite/topic) as the affected person. سر زداد is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'lost his head / his head was given (away)' — i.e., the king was beheaded or his head was taken. The verb phrase is compressed poetically.
segments.s695-g95: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'. برنایی is an adjective/noun meaning 'youthful, a young person'; و links to the next descriptor. نوز 'newborn' (poetic) + نادیده 'unseen/untried' together qualify کار 'deed/actor' — the phrase paints the addressee as inexperienced: 'you, a young and newly untried doer.' Word order places the epithets after the pronoun for emphasis.
segments.s695-g96: چو here functions as 'when/if'. خواهی is the present/future-form of خواستن 'you wish/you will', and the sequence چو خواهی که is a common classical pattern 'when/if you wish that / if you want to'. بر is a prepositional/prefix element attached to the verb یابی to form بر یابی 'to attain/achieve'. از introduces the source 'from' and روزگار here means 'fortune/time/fate' — together: 'when you want to attain (something) from fortune.'
segments.s695-g73: بخواهید is a 2nd‑person plural/formal verbal form from خواستن used here as an exhortation ‘seek/wish/endeavor’. It governs the following noun phrase introduced by بر, which marks the domain or object of the seeking (بر پاکی دین ما = ‘regarding/for the purity of our religion’). Note that پاکی is an abstract noun ‘purity’ and ما is the possessive/subject ‘our’.
segments.s695-g74: چنانچون begins a comparison ‘just as/like’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’ (here referring back to the state of the customs). رسم و آیین ما is the subject in apposition ‘our custom and ritual/practice’. Word order places the comparative and copula before the noun phrase (poetic inversion): ‘just as were our custom and rite’.
segments.s695-g75: بدان is the imperative ‘know’ formed from the prefix ب- + دان; it introduces a temporal clause with تا چو (‘so long as / until when’). تا here gives the temporal boundary ‘as long as’, and چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when/as’, followed by the nominal phrase فرزند قیصر نژاد ‘a descendant/child of Caesar’s race’.
segments.s695-g76: بود is the past copula ‘was’ tying the temporal clause to what follows. The clause کین ایرج نیارد بیاد is an object/result clause: کین ‘vengeance/hatred’ + ایرج (name) and then the verb phrase نیارد بیاد (poetic/archaic formation) meaning roughly ‘would not come to mind / would not be brought to memory’. Here بیاد is the phrase ‘into memory’, and the whole expresses that vengeance toward Iraj would not be recalled; the exact verbal form نیارد is archaic/poetic.
segments.s695-g77: از introduces separation ‘from’ (rest from X). آشوب and جنگ are coordinated nouns ‘turmoil’ and ‘war’; the intervening وز is an archaic/poetic connector functioning like ‘and/of’ here to join the two nouns. روی زمین is a locative phrase ‘on the surface/face of the earth’ (روی = ‘on/upon’, زمین = ‘earth’).
segments.s695-g78: بیاساید is an optative/subjunctive/poetic verb meaning ‘may he be at rest / may he find repose’ (archaic form); it is followed by و ‘and’ to coordinate a second wish. راه جوید بدین = ‘seek a path toward this religion’ where جوید is a present‑subjunctive/optative ‘may he seek’, and ب (here = به shortened) marks direction ‘to/into’ with دین ‘religion’ as its object.
segments.s695-g79: کنون ‘now’ sets the time; چون introduces a temporal clause ‘when/as’. بچشم here is a fused prepositional expression ‘with (the) eye’ (poetic contraction of با چشم) and is linked to خرد ‘wisdom/reason’ to mean ‘with the eye of wisdom’. بنگری is the present‑subjunctive/imperative 2nd singular ‘when you look/consider’.
segments.s695-g80: مراین را is the direct object ‘these things’ with the object marker را. به جز راستی is the prepositional phrase ‘except for / other than truth’ (به جز = ‘except’), and نشمری is negative imperative/subjunctive 2nd singular (‘do not reckon/consider’) formed with negative ن- + شمری; together it gives the instruction ‘do not regard these as anything but the truth’.
segments.s695-g97: مکن is the classical negative imperative 'do not' (here addressing the addressee). The verb comes first for emphasis, followed by the object phrase 'یاری مرد پیمان‌شکن' = 'help a man, an oath-breaker' (مرد = 'man/person'; پیمان‌شکن modifies 'مرد' as an attributive compound). The whole segment means 'Do not help an oath-breaker.'
segments.s695-g98: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for'/'that'). The unusual order 'پیمان‌شکن کس' is a poetic/archaic arrangement meaning 'a person who breaks a covenant' (کس = 'person'); نیرزد is the classical 3rd-person present meaning 'is not worth' or 'does not deserve', and کفن (object) 'shroud' follows the verb. The clause gives the reason: 'for an oath-breaker is not worth a shroud.'
segments.s695-g99: بدان is the imperative 'know (this)' formed from the prefix ب- + دان; it introduces the following clause. شاه is the subject 'a king'; نفرین کند is a classical 3rd-person verb phrase 'brings/utters a curse' (کند here as the classical 3rd-person auxiliary/form of the verb); تاج و گاه are the objects 'crown and (royal) seat' (گاه is used poetically for 'seat/throne'). Word order places the imperative introducer before the declarative clause.
segments.s695-g100: که here begins a relative clause modifying شاه ('a king who ...'). باشد is the classical subjunctive/optative copula ('is / may be') used to state the king's character; و links the two predicates; کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'one who desires vengeance' (کینه = 'hatred/grudge', خواه = 'desirer'). So: 'a king who is an oath-breaker and revenge-seeking.'
segments.s695-g101: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; 'نامه من' is the noun + possessor 'my letter' (Persian normally places the possessed noun before the possessor). سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies the verb, and بخوان is the 2nd-person imperative 'read' (classical imperative form). Together: 'Now read my letter completely.'
segments.s695-g102: گر is the conditional 'if'. انگشتها 'your fingers' (plural), چرب 'greasy' follows the noun as an adjective (postnominal adjective is common in poetry). داری is 2nd-person present 'you have', and مخوان is the negative imperative 'do not read' (م- prefix + خوان). The clause reads: 'If your fingers are greasy, do not read (it).' (i.e. avoid touching the letter with greasy fingers).
segments.s695-g103: سخنها ('the words') is the direct object (plural). نگه‌دار here functions as an imperative 'keep/guard' (classical نگهدار/نگه دار), and و links the clauses. پاسخ نویس is an imperative phrase 'write an answer' (نویس used as a verbal imperative 'write'). The segment instructs: 'Keep the words and write the reply.'
segments.s695-g104: همه 'all/every' modifies خوبی 'goodness/good things'. اندیش is the imperative 'think/consider!' addressing the scribe; و links to the final command. فرخ ('fortunate/auspicious') modifies نویس, where نویس is again an imperative 'write' (so 'write auspiciously' or 'write to bring good fortune'). The sense is: 'Consider all that is good, and write in a fortunate/auspicious manner.'
segments.s695-g105: نخواهم is the 1st‑person negated verb ('I do not want / I will not wish'). که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that'); this clause contains این راز as its object and داند (3sg) as the verb. Note the poetic inversion: the verb داند precedes its agent دبیر (the subject) rather than the prose order دبیر داند.
segments.s695-g106: تو باشی uses تو (you) plus باشی (subjunctive/optative form 'be' used here like an instruction or wish: 'may you be / you should be'). نویسنده تیزویر is a nominal predicate in apposition: نویسنده (scribe/writer) qualified by the poetic epithet تیزویر ('sharp/quick-writing' — an archaic compound epithet).
segments.s695-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برخوانم is 1sg present/subjunctive with the prefix بر‑ ('read aloud' / 'read out'), used here to mark the action that triggers the next clause. این پاسخ‌نامه را is the definite object (را marks the object): 'this reply/answer-letter'.
segments.s695-g108: ببینم is 1sg present/subjunctive ('I see / I will see') following the temporal چو clause. دل مرد خود کامه را is the object phrase (دل = 'heart') with a genitive chain: مرد (man) modified by خود کامه ('self‑willed / satisfied with his own desire'); را again marks the whole as definite object of ببینم. Word order places possessed before possessor in poetic Persian.
segments.s695-g109: همانا is an emphatic discourse particle ('verily / behold') placed before a nominal list. سلیح و سپاه و درم is a coordinated series of nouns ('weapons and army and money'); the conjunction و simply links the series without repeating a verb — a frequent poetic economy.
segments.s695-g110: فرستیم is 1pl verbal form ('we send / we will send') used here as a promise/plan. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). دل نداری is a nominal phrase meaning 'lack of heart' (i.e. the faint‑hearted/cowardly), with نداری as 'lack'. دژم is an adjective 'sullen / dejected' describing the result — together the clause means 'so that the faint‑hearted are put to dejection.'
segments.s695-g111: هرآنکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever'. برتو is a prepositional phrase 'toward you / with respect to you'. گرامی ترست is the predicate: گرامی 'dear/honored' with the comparative marker تر + archaic copula‑ending ست (here fused as ترست) meaning 'is more (dear)'.
segments.s695-g112: وگر is a conditional/adversative 'and if / but if' introducing a parallel alternative. نزد تو 'in your presence / before you'; نیز 'also' adds parallel emphasis; نامی ترست repeats the comparative pattern (نامی 'famous' + ترست 'is more'). The whole line gives two parallel standards: dearer to you and more famous before you.
segments.s695-g9: This is a locative phrase: به قسطنیه places the action at/in Constantinople (به marking direction/place), در reinforces the locative sense ('in/at'), and فراوان modifies سپاه as an adjective meaning 'numerous/abundant'. Poetic word order places the place words before the noun 'سپاه' (army/host).
segments.s695-g10: ندارم is first‑person singular present/past negative 'I do not have' (possess). The clause introduced by که is a subordinate 'that' clause: دارند is third‑person plural 'they have/hold', and کشور نگاه is a noun phrase where نگاه functions as 'keeping/watch/guard' of the country — i.e. 'they hold the country in their keeping'. The whole means 'I do not have (so many troops) that they hold guard of the realm.'
segments.s695-g11: سخنها is the plural definite/collective 'words/tales/speeches'. ز is the poetic preposition 'with/from' marking the manner, هرگونه 'every kind/any manner' modifies the way the words are presented, and آراستیم is first‑person plural past 'we adorned/embellished' (آراستن in the epic past). The phrase means 'we have embellished the tales in every way.'
segments.s695-g12: ز here again means 'from'; هر + کشوری = 'every country/realm' (each land), لشکری is an object meaning 'a military force/forces' and خواستیم is first‑person plural past 'we requested/asked for'. Grammatically this is a simple past clause: 'From every land we requested troops.'
segments.s695-g13: یکایک is a distributive adverb 'one by one / each and every'. چوآیند is a temporal subordinate clause 'چو' = 'when' + آیند (they come) = 'when they come / whenever they arrive'. هم adds emphasis ('indeed/also'), and در زمان 'at the (appointed) time' supplies the temporal frame. Together: 'one by one, whenever they come, at the right time...'.
segments.s695-g14: فرستیم is first‑person plural used here as a future/intentional action 'we will send / we shall dispatch' (classical Persian often uses this form for a future sense). نزدیک تو is a locative phrase 'near you / to your presence' (نزدیک + pronoun تو), and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. The clause states 'we will send (them) to you, without doubt.'
segments.s695-g15: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. مولش (literally 'its/his master') here functions as a substantive 'their lord/patron' or an accompanying noun; و links رای 'counsel/plan'. چندین 'many/several' modifies the following verbal notion, and زدن is used metaphorically ('to strike/compose/offer' plans) — poetic use where 'زدن' expresses forming or enacting a counsel. The clause therefore means 'they all struck/formed many counsels/plans.'
segments.s695-g16: برین is the preposition بر + این (here written as ین) meaning 'upon/against this'. نیشتر (literally 'lancet' or 'sting/point') is used figuratively as 'that sharp point/assault', کام is 'desire/appetite', and شیر آژدن is a compound phrase 'lion‑dragon' (two powerful beasts linked poetically). Grammatically the phrase reads 'against this sting was the lion‑dragon's desire' — i.e. the counsels were directed against that salient danger/urge.
segments.s695-g17: ازان بد is an evaluative phrase: ازان (archaic contracted ازِ آن) + بد meaning “it is bad/unfortunate that…”. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. کردارهای کهن is a noun phrase (کردار + plural suffix‌های) with the adjective کهن following the noun in the normal Persian order, meaning “the ancient deeds.”
segments.s695-g18: همی is a classical frequentative/continuous marker (“kept on / would”), here with یاد کرد (the noun یاد + light verb کرد) to mean “kept recalling / often remembered.” آنک is a relative pronoun “he who / which,” and داند سخن is an archaic word order (verb after subject omitted) meaning “knows speech/language.”
segments.s695-g19: که introduces a temporal clause “when.” هنگام takes a following name to mean “at the time of …”; the sequence شاپور شاه اردشیر lists proper names/titles (classical style often strings names and title appositions). This gives the temporal frame “in the time of Shapur, King Ardashir.”
segments.s695-g20: دلِ مرد is a poetic genitive (ezāfe omitted) meaning “the heart of man / of the hero.” برناشد is an archaic negative past/inchoative-form meaning roughly “was not raised / did not recover.” از رنج سیر is a prepositional phrase “from / because of being sated/weary of suffering,” so the clause means the man’s heart did not recover, worn out by hardship.
segments.s695-g21: ز بس is a poetic equivalent of از بس “because of so much / owing to so much.” The following nouns/infinitives غارت و کشتن و تاختن are listed as actions (nominal or infinitival forms) meaning “plundering, killing, and charging,” with و as simple conjunctions linking them.
segments.s695-g22: به marks the target/recipient (here rendered “against / upon”), بیداد is the noun “injustice/oppression,” برکینها is a plural proper/ethnic or group-name used here as the object of that injustice, and ساختن is the verb “to do/bring about / commit” (classical usage of ساختن = to effect/do). Together: “they committed injustice against the Barakin.”
segments.s695-g23: کزو is a contraction of ازِ او / از آن (“from/of which / from whom”) and بگذری (here classical present/past sense “you pass / passed”) introduces the reference; the following names هرمز و کی قباد are apposed (کی is the royal title “Kay/Key” attached to قباد). The phrase reads as naming Hormuz and Kay Qobad as examples in the foregoing reference.
segments.s695-g24: که introduces a relative clause modifying the named rulers. از داد یزدان is the object phrase “the justice of God/Yazdān” (از marks the domain/concern), نکردند یاد is a classical negative perfect/past construction meaning “they did not remember / they did not make mention of.” So the relative clause says these rulers did not remember God’s justice.
segments.s695-g25: نیای تو is a possessive nominal phrase meaning 'your ancestor' (noun + possessive pronoun after the noun). آن شاه نوشین روان is a noun phrase identifying him: آن 'that', شاه 'king', with two epithets نوشین + روان following the noun (typical Persian adjective order); together نوشین روان = 'sweet-/delightful-souled'.
segments.s695-g26: که introduces a relative clause ('who'). از داد او marks cause/agency ('by/through his justice') where از functions as 'because of / from' and داد means 'justice'. پیر سر شد جوان is a small clause with شد (past of شدن 'become') as the inchoative verb: پیر سر ('the old [in] head' = the aged) + شد جوان 'became young'; the order is poetic but amounts to 'the old became young.'
segments.s695-g27: همه روم is the subject phrase 'the whole of Rome' (همه as 'whole/entire' + روم). ازو 'from/of him' indicates the agent or cause ('because of him'). شد is the past inchoative 'became', and سراسر خراب is the predicate with سراسر as an adverb 'throughout/entirely' modifying the adjective خراب 'ruined'.
segments.s695-g28: چناچون که is a comparative connector 'just as' (چناچون = 'just as'), and the clause that follows is elliptical: ایران ز افراسیاب literally 'Iran from Afrasiab' where ز = 'by/from' (poetic) and the verb (e.g. 'was ruined') is omitted because it is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s695-g29: ازین مرز ما is a prepositional phrase 'from this border of ours' (از + این in contracted form 'ازین', then مرز 'border' + possessive ما 'our'). سی و نه شارستان is the counted noun 'thirty-nine towns' (numeral phrase before the noun). The whole phrase sets the location/extent.
segments.s695-g30: از ایرانیان here marks cause/agent ('by/from the Iranians'). شد is 'became', and همه خارستان is the predicate 'all [became] thorny/wasteland' with همه quantifying خارستان ('wasteland' used figuratively). The verb is simple past inchoative.
segments.s695-g31: ز خون سران uses ز 'from' to indicate cause ('from the blood of the chiefs'), where خون سران is a genitive 'the chiefs' blood'. دشت is the grammatical subject 'the plain' and شد آبگیر is 'became a pool' (شد + predicate آبگیر), a vivid poetic metaphor: the plain turned into a pool of blood.
segments.s695-g32: زن و کودکانشان is a coordinated noun phrase 'women and their children' with شان attached to کودکان but understood to mark the group's possession. ببردند is the past-tense form 'they took / carried off' (the classical b- prefix appears in older Persian forms), and اسیر is the predicative noun 'captive' — together 'they took (them) captive.' The external agent ('they') is not expressed here and is understood from context.
segments.s695-g113: ابا آنک is a concessive connector 'although / despite that' (ابا here is an archaic concessive particle built on the root of 'refrain/reluctance'). آنک introduces the subordinate clause; ز + و is the poetic contraction of از او ('from/of him') with ز = 'from' and the enclitic و = 'him'. کینه داری به دل is a simple predicate 'you have hatred in (your) heart' (داری = present possession).
segments.s695-g114: This is an imperative/admonition introduced by به مردی 'in manhood / like a man' (به + noun used adverbially). ز دل کینه‌ها is a prepositional phrase 'the grudges from the heart'. برگسل here functions as a verbal imperative meaning 'untie / tear away / cast off' (poetic/archaic verb form) directed to the addressee, so the clause commands: 'as a man, cast off the heart's grudges.'
segments.s695-g115: This short sequence groups nouns and adjectives describing the sin: گناهش 'his sin' followed by بیزدان 'godless, without God' qualifying it, and دارنده 'holder/bearer' as an appositive describing the person who bears that quality. بخش here is lexical 'portion/favour' (the next word مکن will negate giving), so this segment prepares the object/idea that will be refused.
segments.s695-g116: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' and here negates the preceding notion (بخش): بخش مکن = 'do not grant/forgive'. روز بر is 'on the day / upon the day' (روز + preposition بر). دشمن و دوست is a coordinated phrase 'enemy and friend'. دخش is a substantive meaning 'splendour / radiance' (or possibly a proper name in some contexts); here it reads as an abstract noun tied to the way favour or honour is shown, so the clause warns against differing treatment toward enemy and friend.
segments.s695-g117: چو خواهی که introduces a conditional/subjunctive clause: چو = 'if' here, خواهی = 'you wish/want', and که introduces the content of the wish. داردت is a third-person present verb + enclitic object 'has/holds you' (here 'may Fortune hold you / may Fortune give you'), and پیروزبخت is the predicate 'fortunate / prosperous' describing what you want Fortune to grant.
segments.s695-g118: This is a coordinated nominal phrase listing the desired status and instruments: جهاندار 'world-ruler / sovereign' is the title wished for, و با لشکر و تاج و تخت lists accompaniment and symbols ('and with an army and crown and throne'). با is the preposition 'with' linking the instruments/means to the desired state.
segments.s695-g119: زچیزکسان is a genitive/compound poetic construction meaning 'from the things/possessions of small (poor) people' (ز = from/of + چیزکسان = 'the things/people of small means'). دست کوتاه دار is an idiomatic directive 'keep your hand short' = refrain from taking or reaching out (i.e., do not seize from them). دار here is the verb 'have/keep' used in an imperative-like counseling idiom.
segments.s695-g120: روان را is the definite object/topic 'the soul' (روان) plus the object marker را. سوی راستی راه دار is an exhortation: سوی = 'toward', راستی = 'rightness/truth', راه = 'path/way', and دار again functions as 'keep/hold' (i.e., 'set/keep your soul on the path of rightness'). The clause is a moral imperative: direct your inner self to upright conduct.
segments.s695-g41: به افسون is an adverbial/prepositional phrase: به ('by') + افسون ('enchantment/charm'), meaning 'by enchantment.' دل مردمان is a possessive NP ('the hearts of the people') with the noun دل ('heart') followed by the plural possessive مردمان ('people'). پاک شد is a past-tense/copular use of شدن ('became/was made clean') — here 'became purified.' The clause order places the adverbial before the subject NP and closes with the compound predicate 'پاک شد.'
segments.s695-g42: همه is the subject 'everyone/all.' زهر گیرنده is a compound: زهر ('poison') + گیرنده (active participle 'receiver/one who takes'), i.e. 'those who take poison.' تریاک شد uses شد (شدن) as 'became' with تریاک ('opium'); literally 'became opium' — idiomatically here 'became as if under opium / became opiated/inebriated.' The verb-final construction (object/phrase before شد) is normal in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s695-g43: بدان برنهادم is a compound verbal phrase: بدان here functions roughly as 'to/with that' (به + آن) — 'with that' or 'to that purpose' — and برنهادم is the past tense 'I placed/established' (برنهادن). کزین درسخن is a prepositional/relative stretch: کزین (archaic contraction of که از این) = 'from this' and درسخن = 'in speech / in this account.' Overall: 'I set/established this so that, from this account...'.
segments.s695-g44: This clause expresses the intended result/purpose. نگوید here is negative-jussive/optative sense 'may not speak / should not speak' (Classical Persian uses subjunctive forms like this to express prohibition/wish), کس = 'anyone,' از = 'of/regarding,' روزگار کهن = 'ancient times/olden days.' Word order places the verb (negated jussive) before the subject کس for emphasis: 'that no one should speak of ancient times.'
segments.s695-g45: به چیزی = 'concerning/for anything' (به + چیزی). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/which'; گویی تو here is the clause's verb phrase meaning 'you say' (goyi as 'you say' / 'that you say') and فرمان کنم is a first-person verb phrase 'I will command / I will make (it) an order.' In classical Persian, کنم (subjunctive/present) often carries a future or volitional sense: 'I shall/may do.' The whole segment: 'for whatever thing you say, I shall issue the command.'
segments.s695-g46: روان را is the definite object 'the soul' (با را marking definite object/emphasis). به پیمان گروگان کنم is the verb phrase: گروگان کردن = 'to take/hold as hostage/pledge' and به پیمان indicates the hostage is held 'to the covenant' (as guarantee). کنَم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used with volitional/future sense: 'I will make/take.' Word order (object before verb) is normal in Persian.
segments.s695-g47: شما را زبان داد is literally 'I gave you a tongue' — an idiom meaning 'I gave you the right/ability to speak' or 'I gave you the words.' شِما را (object before noun) + زبان (speech/utterance) + داد (past 'gave'). باید همان is a modal clause: باید = 'must/ought' and همان = 'the same/that very thing' — 'it must be the same.' The structure states an obligation about the speech given to 'you.'
segments.s695-g48: که introduces a purpose/result clause: 'so that.' بر ما نباشد کسی بدگمان is literal 'that no one be suspicious of us' — بر ما = 'toward us/regarding us,' نباشد uses the negative subjunctive/optative 'may not be / should not be,' کسی = 'anyone,' بدگمان = 'suspicious/doubtful.' The clause expresses the intended outcome: the speech must be such that no one will harbor suspicion against us.

sentences.s695-s61: When the heading of that letter came back bare, thereupon they produced a little barberry seal.
sentences.s695-s62: Thereupon the Caesar set a seal; he gave the gem to the messenger and uttered praise (i.e. praised him).
sentences.s695-s1: Then someone quickly wrote a letter; thereupon he cried out "Afarin! afarin!" and increased the praise.
sentences.s695-s2: That, together with the mobed, united in heart and pure in counsel, we made a decision, separating bad from good and rejecting impure counsel.
sentences.s695-s3: From every kind of tale we recited (or composed) stories; on that counsel (or plan) we returned to former times.
sentences.s695-s4: Now that opinions and utterances have arisen—behold—I opened them in the ancient treasuries.
sentences.s695-s29: Whenever there was any matter concerning you, or if it was an offensive or warlike affair.
sentences.s695-s30: We would all become friends and brothers; yet it sometimes happened that we became the lesser.
sentences.s695-s31: When our town became self-sufficient, all hatred would rise up in your hearts.
sentences.s695-s32: Talk arose about Tur and Salm concerning those vain, ancient days.
sentences.s695-s41: Let there remain from the bond of our covenant; from God, such is our command.
sentences.s695-s42: From the time of Piruz to Khushnawaz — verily a long year passed.
sentences.s695-s43: For both gave up their heads and were lost; may the sovereign not be an oath-breaker himself.
sentences.s695-s44: Christ the Prophet thus recalled that the intellect turns when it is given a turn.
sentences.s695-s17: If a Roman man harbors hatred in his heart, you ought not to be surprised by that marvel.
sentences.s695-s18: Hurting oneself is not in our religion; may it never become our custom to do evil.
sentences.s695-s19: We have seen nothing better than uprightness; that is, keeping away from wrong and deficiency.
sentences.s695-s20: I summoned all the oppressed, and with weighty words I spoke at length.
sentences.s695-s33: Now one pledge must be made firm, worthy as a token of love.
sentences.s695-s34: From this occasion let us not speak of Irāj's grudge, nor of the old times.
sentences.s695-s35: From now on let Iran and Rome be one; let us not seek separation of this border and land.
sentences.s695-s36: Then our household has one daughter, who is wiser than the elders.
sentences.s695-s25: Say (it): until I win the ruler's land, I will not submit to such weak and humiliating hardships.
sentences.s695-s26: I do not desire even the Romans' acclaim, nor will I sell these hardships for anything.
sentences.s695-s27: As for anything else you possess of that frontier and land, let no one from Iran hand over the border to Rome.
sentences.s695-s28: Pursue this wish further; make peace with us and enter into kinship.
sentences.s695-s45: The pleasant minstrel devised many plans among them so that the victor's head would not come to harm from a blow.
sentences.s695-s46: When Pirooz treated him harshly, he saw in that place a dark smoke.
sentences.s695-s47: That army and the royal throne were swept away; they turned (fled), and the king lost his head.
sentences.s695-s48: You are a young man and a newborn untried doer; when you wish to gain (something) from fortune...
sentences.s695-s37: Seek to preserve the purity of our religion, just as our custom and ritual have been.
sentences.s695-s38: Know this: so long as there was a descendant of Caesar’s line, the hatred toward Iraj would not come to mind.
sentences.s695-s39: May he be at rest from turmoil and war upon the earth, and may he seek a way to this faith.
sentences.s695-s40: Now, when you look with the eye of wisdom, count these things as nothing but truth.
sentences.s695-s49: Do not help an oath-breaker; an oath-breaker is not even worth a shroud.
sentences.s695-s50: Know that a king who breaks his oath and seeks revenge will bring a curse upon crown and throne.
sentences.s695-s51: Now read my letter all the way through; if your fingers are greasy, do not read it.
sentences.s695-s52: Keep the words and write an answer; consider every good thing and write auspiciously.
sentences.s695-s53: I do not want your secretary to learn this secret — you yourself should be the quick-writing scribe.
sentences.s695-s54: When I read this reply, I will see the heart of the self-willed man.
sentences.s695-s55: Verily, we will send weapons, an army and money so that the faint‑hearted may be put to dejection.
sentences.s695-s56: Whoever is more honored toward you, and whoever is more renowned in your presence.
sentences.s695-s5: I do not have in Constantinople a plentiful host that keeps watch over the realm.
sentences.s695-s6: I/we have adorned the words in every manner; from every land we asked for troops.
sentences.s695-s7: One by one, whenever they come, at the appointed time, we will send them to your presence without doubt.
sentences.s695-s8: All of them devised many counsels (and) struck numerous plans; against that sting was the dragon‑lion's desire.
sentences.s695-s9: It was unfortunate that he who knows speech kept recalling the deeds of old.
sentences.s695-s10: When, in the time of Shapur, King Ardashir, men's hearts would not be restored, weary from suffering.
sentences.s695-s11: Because of so much plundering and killing and charging, they perpetrated injustice against the Barakin (the hostile ones).
sentences.s695-s12: From these passed Hormuz and Kay Qobad — who did not remember the justice of Yazdān (God).
sentences.s695-s13: Your forefather was that sweet-souled king, who by his justice made the aged become young.
sentences.s695-s14: All of Rome became entirely ruined because of him, just as Iran (was) by Afrasiab.
sentences.s695-s15: From this frontier of ours thirty-nine towns — by the Iranians they all became wasteland.
sentences.s695-s16: From the blood of the chiefs the plain became a pool; women and their children were taken captive.
sentences.s695-s57: Although you harbour hatred of him in your heart, as a man tear those grudges away from your heart.
sentences.s695-s58: Count his sin as that of one without God; do not grant (it) — do not, on that day, show favour (or splendour) differently to enemy or friend.
sentences.s695-s59: If you wish that Fortune make you fortunate — a world-ruler with army, crown and throne —
sentences.s695-s60: Keep your hand short with regard to petty people's possessions; steer your soul toward the path of rectitude.
sentences.s695-s21: By enchantment the hearts of the people were purified; all who took poison became as if under opium.
sentences.s695-s22: I set this down so that, in this account, no one should speak of ancient times.
sentences.s695-s23: For whatever you say I will give an order; I will hold the soul as a hostage to the covenant.
sentences.s695-s24: I gave you speech; it must be the same, so that no one be suspicious of us.
